body.title: Ground Loop — the minimal always-alive waker/recoverer (NOT a supervisor)
body.summary: Ground Loop's ONLY job is keeping ITSELF alive so it can wake others as needed: wake a thing up when it is called for, recover things from crashes, and launch Claude to repair when something is broken. It is the bottom-most liveness floor of the rack, deliberately tiny so it is the one thing that is always up. Akien's correction (2026-06-24): it is NOT a process supervisor and the word 'supervisor' in older notes/decisions (D-ground-loop-2026-06-13) is the wrong frame — it does not own or hold a fixed set of long-lived children, it wakes/recovers them on demand. Scheduling does NOT live here (that is Nanny Ogg); Ground Loop at most provides a heartbeat that kicks the scheduler. Reading the descriptor-scan + plugin_proxy/plugin_daemon behavior through this lens: those are wake-and-keep-reachable mechanics, not a long-term child-supervision contract.

key_concepts.name: prime directive
key_concepts.what: keep ITSELF alive — that is the whole point; everything else is downstream of always being up to wake/recover others

key_concepts.name: wake on demand
key_concepts.what: bring a device/plugin up when it is called for; do not hold a fixed roster of running children

key_concepts.name: crash recovery
key_concepts.what: restart a thing that died; for code-level breakage, launch Claude to repair

key_concepts.name: not the scheduler
key_concepts.what: the schedule lives in Nanny Ogg; Ground Loop or the OS crontab only emit a heartbeat to tick her

anti_patterns: Calling Ground Loop a 'supervisor' / assuming it holds children in its own process tree forever.
Putting schedules or cron logic in Ground Loop — that is Nanny Ogg's job (platform-independent scheduling).
Growing Ground Loop with features — every added responsibility risks the one thing it must never lose: staying alive.
